1: baYHWH ḥāsîtî (ḇaYHWH ḥāsîṯî): 'In the LORD I take refuge.' The opening confession — baYHWH ḥāsîtî — is the psalm's thesis. The root ḥāsâ ('to take refuge, flee for protection') is used extensively in the Psalter for covenant trust; it implies running to a physically stronger protector. The advice of unnamed advisors (ʾîmrû lĕnapšî nûdî harkĕm ṣippōrĕkem, 'flee like a bird to your mountain!') is rejected before it is quoted. The psalm's genre has been called a 'confidence psalm' — the trust expressed in v.1 needs no argument; it is the starting point from which everything else follows.
2: kî hinnēh rĕšāʿîm yidrekûn qešet (kî hinnēh rĕšāʿîm yiḏreḵûn qāšeṯ): 'for behold, the wicked bend the bow.' The verb dārak ('to tread/bend') applied to the bow describes arming for attack. The imagery of arrow shot bĕmô-ʾōpel, 'in the dark,' emphasizes treachery — the attack is hidden, impossible to defend against. The target is lĕyišrê-lēb, 'at the upright in heart.' The contrast — wicked with weapons / righteous with no defence — creates the crisis that makes the question of v.3 so pressing.
3: kî hāšĕtôt yehāressûn mah-pāʿal ṣaddîq (kî hāšŝtôṯ yehārēsûn mah-pāʿal ṣaddîq): 'when the foundations are destroyed, what can the righteous do?' The noun šātôt ('foundations, pillars') — from šît, 'to set, establish' — refers to the moral and social structures that make ordered life possible. The rhetorical question mah-pāʿal ṣaddîq? ('what can the righteous do?') is the voice of despair: if the foundations collapse, no human action suffices. The psalm's answer is implicit: what the righteous do is precisely what v.1 declares — take refuge in YHWH, the one on whose throne the foundations ultimately rest.
4: YHWH bĕhêkal qodšô YHWH baššāmayim kissĕʾô (YHWH bĕhêḵal qodšô YHWH baššāmayim kissĕʾô): 'The LORD is in his holy temple; the LORD — his throne is in heaven.' The two-fold affirmation — earthly temple and heavenly throne — is programmatic: YHWH simultaneously inhabits the Jerusalem sanctuary (locus of covenant presence) and the heavenly court (locus of sovereign judgment). The participle bōḥēn ('he tests/examines') combined with ʿênāyw yĕḥĕzû ('his eyes see') directly answers the wicked man's claim in Ps 10:11 that God neither sees nor acts. Divine omniscience is the foundation that the wicked man's practical atheism cannot survive.
5: YHWH ṣaddîq yibḥān wĕrāšāʿ wĕʾōhēb ḥāmās śānĕʾâh napšô (YHWH ṣaddîq yiḇḥān wĕrāšāʿ wĕʾōhēḇ ḥāmās śānĕʾāh napšô): 'The LORD tests the righteous, but the wicked and the one who loves violence his soul hates.' The verb bāḥan ('test, examine, refine') — used of metallic refinement — indicates that YHWH's scrutiny of the righteous is purifying, not merely punitive. The phrase napšô śānĕʾâh ('his soul hates') applied to the wicked is strong: divine hate is not emotional pique but settled moral revulsion at evil. The 'one who loves violence' (ʾōhēb ḥāmās) identifies the wicked by affection rather than merely behavior.
6: yamṭēr ʿal rĕšāʿîm paḥîm (yamṭēr ʿal-rĕšāʿîm paḥîm): 'he rains snares on the wicked.' The divine judgment is pictured as a storm of paḥîm ('snares, traps'), ʾēš ('fire'), wĕgāprit ('brimstone'), and rûaḥ zîlĕʿôt ('a scorching wind'). The sulfur-and-fire imagery recalls Sodom (Gen 19:24) — the paradigmatic judgment on cities that exchanged hospitality and justice for violence. The phrase zōt mĕnat kôsām, 'this is the portion of their cup,' uses kôs ('cup') as a metonym for fate or divine allotment — the wicked receive what their choices earned.
7: kî ṣaddîq YHWH ṣĕdāqôt ʾāhēb (kî ṣaddîq YHWH ṣĕdāqôt ʾāhēḇ): 'for the LORD is righteous; he loves righteous deeds.' The final verse resolves the crisis of v.3: because YHWH himself is ṣaddîq ('righteous'), he necessarily loves ṣĕdāqôt ('acts of righteousness'). The wicked foundations (v.3) cannot finally stand because righteousness is ontologically grounded in YHWH's character. The concluding phrase yāšār yĕḥĕzû pānêmô, 'the upright shall behold his face,' is the psalm's beatific vision — the reward of those who flee to YHWH (v.1) is intimacy with him, seeing the face that the wicked cannot bear (cf. Rev 22:4; Matt 5:8).
